original_abstract: To restore communication for individuals with paralysis, speech brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) aim to translate neural activity into text or audible speech. While prior research has established the potential for such systems, achieving high-speed, large-vocabulary decoding remains a significant hurdle. In this study, we present an intracortical speech-to-text BCI that utilizes high-resolution spiking activity from microelectrode arrays. By recording from the ventral premotor cortex (area 6v) of a participant with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) who can no longer speak intelligibly, we achieved a word error rate of 9.1% on a 50-word vocabulary—a 2.7-fold improvement over previous benchmarks. More importantly, we demonstrate the first successful decoding of a 125,000-word vocabulary, achieving a word error rate of 23.8%. The BCI facilitated communication at an average speed of 62 words per minute, significantly surpassing prior records and approaching the rates of natural conversation. Our neurophysiological findings reveal that area 6v contains a robust, spatially intermixed representation of speech articulators, and that the articulatory code for phonemes remains preserved years after the loss of functional speech. These results provide a proof-of-concept for a high-performance neuroprosthesis capable of restoring rapid, unconstrained communication to people with severe speech disabilities.
humanized_abstract: Brain-computer interface (BCI) technology has the potential to give back communication to people with paralysis. Speech BCIs translate brain signals into text or speech. While BCIs have been shown to be viable, they generally lack high speed, large vocabulary, accurate speech, or have suffered from a number of limitations. Our work presents an intracortical speech-to-text BCI capable of translating high resolution, fine grained spiking activity from microelectrode arrays into text. The recordings were extracted from the ventral premotor cortex (area 6v) of an articulate and motivated 43 year old female subject with ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis), an incurable neurodegenerative disease that invades motor neurons throughout the body except for those that control voluntary speech, leaving speech preservation until very late in the progression of the disease, or in a very small number of cases, until its complete absence. The word error rate (WER) on our current 50 word vocabulary is 9.1% (2.7x faster than the prior record for accurate speech) and our results show, for the first time, that it is possible to read and write a 125,000 word dictionary with a WER of 24% or 23.8% after pruning a list of over 1,300 most frequently repeated word pairs that BCI users are expected to face for their whole lifetime. Our results show that this BCI is capable of conveying communication at a speed of 62 words per minute on average, or faster than that of the conversation that the subject currently communicates at, using only gazing. Our study demonstrates that a BCI based on this principle is capable of leading to a fully natural neuroprosthetic communication platform. Importantly, our results not only provide some of the first information about the detailed organization of human brain functions behind speech articulation; they also strongly suggest that area 6v can act as an almost instant placeholder for lost speech even in cases of years of complete lack of speech articulation.
